risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "After all assessments, included patients were randomised by the Karolinska Trial Alliance, an independent clinical trial research organisation where a computer program generated the group allocation, with the size of the randomisation blocks (36 patients) being unknown to the researchers." Comment: This describes a computer-generated allocation sequence with randomisation blocks and independent trial management, which is sufficient to indicate a truly random sequence was used.

Quote: "After all assessments, included patients were randomised by the Karolinska Trial Alliance, an independent clinical trial research organisation where a computer program generated the group allocation, with the size of the randomisation blocks (36 patients) being unknown to the researchers. When the research nurse had entered all assessment data into the study database and confirmed that all inclusion criteria were met, the allocation for the new patient was revealed via the user interface of the database." Comment: The allocation was handled by an independent trial organisation and was only revealed after eligibility/data entry through a database interface, which indicates a concealed central allocation mechanism preventing recruiters from foreseeing the next assignment.

risk_of_bias.domain: Blinding of participants and personnel (performance bias)
risk_of_bias.support_text: Summary: The study used three clearly distinguishable interventions, including a face-to-face exercise program and a therapist-supported internet CBT program, versus treatment as usual. The report does not describe any blinding of participants or personnel, and the nature of the interventions makes blinding implausible. Because key outcomes included self-reported employment status, sick leave, and work ability, knowledge of allocation could plausibly influence reporting and performance.

Quote: "Primary outcomes were based on patients’ self-ratings at baseline and at 3-month and 12-month follow-up." and "One main question: ‘What is your current employment status?’" and "The study aimed to evaluate ... on self-rated work ability". Comment: The outcome assessor was the patient themselves (self-report), not a blinded independent assessor. Because employment status, sick leave, and work ability were self-rated and participants could know their treatment allocation, knowledge of allocation could plausibly influence reporting and thus outcome measurement. This makes detection bias likely.

Quote: "Of the 703 patients included in the analyses of sick leave, 514 (73%) valid measures were obtained at 3-month follow-up and 539 (77%) at 12 months. ... χ2 tests revealed no differences in attrition between arms besides for the sick leave at 3 months, where the groups differed significantly with TAU having an attrition of 33% compared with 27% for PE and 21% for ICBT." Comment: Although attrition was not trivial, it was moderate and generally balanced, with only one time point showing a statistically significant difference and no indication that missingness was related to the outcome in a way that would obviously bias results. The authors also used methods that accommodate missing data (GEE and mixed models) and performed sensitivity analyses that gave equivalent results, which supports a low-risk judgement.
